text: Heavy slow resistance training produces good short-term and long-term clinical effects in patellar tendinopathy, with maintained improvements in function and pain at 6-month follow-up.
Corticosteroid injections provide good short-term relief in patellar tendinopathy but clinical effects deteriorate by 6-month follow-up.
Eccentric decline squat training maintains improvements in VISA-p scores and VAS pain ratings at 6-month follow-up in patellar tendinopathy.
Heavy slow resistance training reduces patellar tendon swelling by approximately 12% and tendon vascularization by approximately 45% after 12 weeks of treatment.
Heavy slow resistance training elevates collagen network turnover in patellar tendons, suggesting structural remodeling and pathology improvement.
Patients with patellar tendinopathy report higher treatment satisfaction with heavy slow resistance training compared to eccentric decline squats or corticosteroid injections at 6-month follow-up.

Tendon mechanical properties in patellar tendinopathy are similar between healthy and injured tendons and are not affected by corticosteroid, eccentric, or heavy slow resistance interventions over 12 weeks.
